posts.name: Andy Ma
posts.cooked: Just my opinion, with all that’s going on in the world, I think it would be crazy to assume that the lowest price you’ll see in the US on release is $195. Comtes is historically a value tete de cuvee, is not supply constrained like Salon (although I don’t know what their relative yields in 2008 were), and does not appear to be experiencing a sudden and dramatic surge in interest. There is nothing to justify a 50-60% increase in price from a moderate QPR 2007. It’s a highly anticipated release among a small minority of wine nerds. Maybe that drives it up to $140 or $150. As in all cases, pick your spots and you should be able to grab it for less than the initial release price. I think Barolo 2016 is a decent corollary and prices are up 5-10% from 2015 if you discard the most opportunistic retailers.

posts.name: Alex Willi@mson
posts.cooked:
There’s a real problem for Taittinger with Comtes. Quality is high and production is reasonably big (well over a large Bordeaux estate).

The wine is only sold to winos and restaurants. Never in airplanes (back when they were still a thing - imagine how much DomP Emirates got through daily pre-covid), never in nightclubs, never in random stores that keep an expensive bottle back for when someone comes in with money to burn… this means not enough gets drunk.

Taittinger are currently selling the 05, 06 and 07 through merchants onto the “secondary” market. That’s why UK merchant pricing is so homogenous across those vintages.

Until the marketing department get their act together, Comtes is doomed to be cheap and oversupplied to people like us.

C Heidsieck’s 12 straight vintage was just offered at the same price as 06 Comtes and above 07… at some point the ocean of undrunk Comtes will Start harming the rest of champagne’s ability to sell to fine wine drinkers

posts.name: Mike Hawkins
posts.cooked:
There’s a real problem for Taittinger with Comtes. Quality is high and production is reasonably big (well over a large Bordeaux estate).

The wine is only sold to winos and restaurants. Never in airplanes (back when they were still a thing - imagine how much DomP Emirates got through daily pre-covid), never in nightclubs, never in random stores that keep an expensive bottle back for when someone comes in with money to burn… this means not enough gets drunk.

Taittinger are currently selling the 05, 06 and 07 through merchants onto the “secondary” market. That’s why UK merchant pricing is so homogenous across those vintages.

Until the marketing department get their act together, Comtes is doomed to be cheap and oversupplied to people like us.

C Heidsieck’s 12 straight vintage was just offered at the same price as 06 Comtes and above 07… at some point the ocean of undrunk Comtes will Start harming the rest of champagne’s ability to sell to fine wine drinkers

Not entirely true. A couple of observations… Comtes has been served in Qantas First Class for many years and has helped that airline win many wine awards.

Secondly, Pierre Emmanuel has said on a few occasions (I’ve heard it directly from him) that he didn’t want to continually raise prices Like other French producers (a) to maintain customer loyalty … and that isn’t the nightclub crowd and (b) as a byproduct of ‘a’ , to ensure these customers are retained so that the business in turn can remain in family hands.

That said, you make some very valid points.

Cheers

Mike

posts.name: R@y.Tupp@+sch

CdC’s production is not “well over a large Bordeaux estate”.

posts.name: Brad Baker
posts.cooked:
In most recent vintages, Comtes production has been fairly steady at a production of around 500k-550k bottles of BdB and Rose combined with the BdB being the large majority. I think the consecutive releases of 2004, 2005, 2006 at high production volume + a decent volume of 2007 has put a lot of product in the market. Both the BdB and Rose were made in all four of these years. Taittinger also had a run of consecutive vintages from 1993-2000, but the Rose was not made in every year (no 94 and 98) and some years were very limited (94 and 97 BdB).

The supply in the market has been affecting Comtes for a little while now. They were behind in releasing the 2007 Comtes by almost two years and are now over two and a half years behind releasing the 2008 Comtes (vs. the original plan). I’m not saying that the wines are not benefiting from some of the extra time aging at the winery on/off the lees, but it will be interesting to see how the market demand treats the current supply. For my dollar, Comtes represents one of the great buys in Champagne and is a bargain at its often seen $100-$130 price point for currently released vintages.

*Edited to correctly state that 2007 Comtes is not at a high/full volume production level like 2004-2006.

posts.name: Alex Willi@mson
posts.cooked:
Not entirely true. A couple of observations… Comtes has been served in Qantas First Class for many years and has helped that airline win many wine awards.

Secondly, Pierre Emmanuel has said on a few occasions (I’ve heard it directly from him) that he didn’t want to continually raise prices Like other French producers (a) to maintain customer loyalty … and that isn’t the nightclub crowd and (b) as a byproduct of ‘a’ , to ensure these customers are retained so that the business in turn can remain in family hands.

That said, you make some very valid points.

Cheers

Mike

Sorry you’re quite right re:Qantas, but of course QF only flew a limited amount of first class (Essentially the A380s) and Comtes wasn’t their only house fizz in F, they also served Grande Dame.

I don’t understand how having 20+ UK merchants all up-to-their eyeballs in Comtes vintages initially released over 5 years ago is consistent with customer loyalty. It would certainly appear consistent with having only one distribution channel and not trying hard enough to help them to put product into the hands of people who drink it rather than park it in warehouses. Even this year Taittinger are actively trying to prevent the secondary market pricing of older Comtes being lower because they still want to sell the same wines and want a homogenous market price.

All is definitely not well with Comtes’ distribution strategy. I love the wines but I know a broken business model when I see one.

posts.name: Alex Willi@mson
posts.cooked:
Dom is very popular in the night scene

Yep, hence the “Luminous” glow in the dark bottlings (LVMH assure me it’s exactly the same wine in there and they don’t change the blend or dosage for the market). Certainly not for purists, but if you happen to be in a nightclub at 2am (in a different world to today), it’s kinder to your tastebuds than tequila …

posts.name: Michael Bowden
posts.cooked: Does Taittinger do a late release/RD/Oenotheque/P2/long vieillissement for the CdC?

posts.name: Brad Baker
posts.cooked:
Does Taittinger do a late release/RD/Oenotheque/P2/long vieillissement for the CdC?

Michael,

Yes, but it isn’t advertised and is still more of a special request type of thing. Going back over a decade, there have been talks of launching something officially, but it hasn’t happened yet. Historically, the Taittinger family really didn’t hold a lot of wines back. They do have some (both disgorged and undisgorged), but for most vintages prior to 2000 (Maybe they could do a very limited release of some mid-90s vintages), they are not in quantities to take to market with an official release like a DP, Roederer, Clicquot, Bollinger, etc… I expect this will change at some point, but I thought that five years ago as well.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz

At $185, I am not a buyer. I say that as a loyal Taittinger customer, with ten plus cases in the cellar. I can backfill with other Champagnes to my heart’s content, and will if it is released at more than $135.

This is not a product that you can generally raise prices 50% and hope to get away with it. Way too much competition, and frankly Champagne sales have been clobbered by the virus, so it is an even less friendly environment to mess around with pricing.

I generally buy Champagne in England, but the dollar pound relationship has not been good lately, so even that may be a little too pricey.

Score?

There’s a review of a preview disgorgement on our site, and one of the commercial disgorgement publishing this month (spoiler: there wasn’t much difference between the two).

In the context of some of the 2008 prestige cuvées released so far, I would rank the Comtes behind Cristal Rosé; on a similar level to Cristal; a touch ahead of Bollinger Grande Année and Pol Roger Churchill; and a couple of points ahead of the 2008 Dom Pérignon. But, this is to compare chalk and cheese, as all these bottlings have very different styles. Of course, there are a few 2008 releases to come, but I think it’s clear that Comtes numbers among the best of all the Grandes Marques’ offerings in this vintage. At some point, I’m looking forward to tasting them all side by side over an afternoon…

La Fete du Champagne 2019 - WINE TALK - WineBerserkers

I think I’ve had ‘08 Dom 3 times, and on each occasion it took a couple hours to really get going. So I’m skeptical of a PnP taste at Fete. (But now it’s slumber time. If you want try more mature Dom, I can pop a ‘96 at our postponed joint Bday.)

For the same reason as above, I’ve reserved on my relatively meh PnP impressions of ‘09 and ‘12 Cristal from the annual Crush tasting. Pop and pour doesn’t always work like it did that first time we had ‘08 Cristal at Fete 2018.

Any hoo, I’m looking forward to the next time we can actually break bread without staring into the camera on my tablet!

posts.name: Alex Willi@mson
posts.cooked:
I don’t expect a significant UK wholesale price bump from the 07s to the 08s. The 2002 CdC price spiked on release because it was an extremely small CdC production that year and there had been no 2001 produced before it - they were selling a small amount into an eager and recently undersupplied market. the previous vintage on the market was the '00 which was pretty atypical too (I love it but that doesn’t appear to have been a consensus view).

The 2008 was a normal sized production and is being released late because Taittinger are sitting on an ocean of unsold 2005, 2006 and 2007 CdC - they even tried to move some of the 07s by guaranteeing 1:1 allocations of 08 to 07 buyers. Much like Cristal 2008 the house will be selling more and more of the 08 in the secondary market for quite a while so if you don’t like Day 1 pricing, just wait a few months and see.

posts.name: Brad Baker
posts.cooked:
The problem with the 2008 Dom (and a lot of Dom when young) is that the Dom character rules out. Do a blind tasting of 2008 vs. 2009 Dom alongside a blind tasting of 2008 vs. 2009 Cristal. It is very easy to pick out the vintage of Cristal as the vintage character is strong and the Cristal character is secondary. With Dom, it is actually difficult to tell the 2008 and 2009 apart. The Dom character far overpowers the vintage character of 2008 and 2009. Yes, you can tell them apart, but it takes some concentration especially when done side by side with Cristal where the differences between 2008 and 2009 are huge. I have seen many mix up the 2008 and 2009 Dom vintages. As Dom ages, the vintage characteristics come out a bit more, but the Dom character is more and more front and center now.

As for Comtes, the wine has a character, but the vintage always shows through as well. I saw both some Comtes, Dom, and Cristal. You can’t go wrong that way.

posts.name: Michael S. Monie
posts.cooked:
Have they ever priced north of Dom? That would be bold…

Why? It’s smaller production and often better.

Is there any tete de cuvée that has a larger production than DP? Knowing that many producers cut back production of their 2008’s because of the economy at the time, does anyone know what the quantity of the 2008 CdC will be?

posts.name: Brad Baker
posts.cooked:
Have they ever priced north of Dom? That would be bold…

Why? It’s smaller production and often better.

Is there any tete de cuvée that has a larger production than DP? Knowing that many producers cut back production of their 2008’s because of the economy at the time, does anyone know what the quantity of the 2008 CdC will be?

No, and it isn’t even close. DP produces more than most Champagne producers make across their entire range. There is usually more DP made in a vintage than what Roderer, Bollinger, Ruinart, Pol Roger, Charles Heidsieck, and Perrier-Jouet each make across all their wines in a year. In fact there are only 10-11 producers in all of Champagne that make more wine than DP does.

In terms of prestige wine comparisons, DP is normally larger by a factor of 6x-8x over the next highest (can vary by year) and often at 10x or more when compared to most of the other big names (Comtes, Grand Siecle, Cristal, Grande Dame, Belle Epoque, Dom Ruinart, Krug Vintage, Grande Annee, etc…). As LVMH continues to acquire top notch land and spread it around the companies in the group, I expect this gap may even get larger with future releases.

2008 was a normal vintage of Comtes so around ~500k bottles. Also, the economic impact in Champagne was felt much more with the 2009 vintage.

Have sales of higher end Champagnes like the ones being discussed here gone down or is it just sales of lower end mediocre Champagnes that have been impacted. Over a number of years, sales of wines in a number of European regions have suffered, but not the top wines. Bordeaux is a great example. Sales of average Bordeaux have suffered for years, but the prices of the top wines have soared and they are in as much or more demand as ever. But, Champagne could be different. Is there anywhere in the world where the quantities made of top wines (like Dom, Comte de Champagne, etc.) are so huge?

posts.name: Justin Bonner
posts.cooked:
Why would one not get 2008 Dom at lower price? Heck I would pay little bit more and get Cristal.

I think the 2008 Dom is overrated, personally, so it’s far from a universal opinion (subject to tasting the 2008 Comtes). I agree the Cristal is good, but it’s also priced considerably higher, which is partially the point - at half the price of Cristal, Comtes is great. At the price of Cristal…

Couldn’t disagree more on the 08 Dom. Have had it at least 8-10 times and alongside 08 Cristal on multiple occasions. While 08 Cristal has consistently bested the Dom, they’re fairly close and IMO, the 08 Dom is the best vintage I can remember in decades. But that 08 Cristal…legendary.
